criteria.criterion_text: There is a system to advise patients of the process for the follow up of results.
A documented system to identify, follow up and recall patients with clinically significant results is implemented.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Compliance will be verified through documented policies, patient record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: When patients are informed of results over the telephone, the patient is identified with 3 approved identifiers before the information is given.
criteria.guideline: This should be in accordance with practice policy. A record should be available as evidence which must include the 3 identifiers as specified in the practice policy, the date results were issued and the name of the personnel who issued results.

standards.standard_title: There is a process for appropriate referral of patients for specialised consultation/investigations at other healthcare facilities.
standards.intent:
In some cases, radiologists may refer patients for a secondary consultation to confirm an opinion, or to request more extensive diagnostic evaluations than may be available at the diagnostic imaging practice, or to have patients receive specialised treatment that the referring practice may be unable to provide.  The practice must clearly describe the referral process.  Practices may wish to consider the use of a standard referral form which includes a tear off slip for the receiving doctor's response following the consultation. The referral form should contain the following information:
at least three approved patient identifiers
relevant history, examination findings and current management
known allergies, adverse drug reactions and current medication
the name of the referring doctor
stated purpose of the referral
request for feedback following the consultation with the specialist

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented process to refer patients.
The lines of communication between the practice, referral centres and community services are clearly defined.
criteria.guideline: Practice personnel must clearly describe the referral process, especially where patients are sent to another healthcare organisation for specialist consultation or special investigations and then return to the referring practice for further investigation. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The doctor informs the patient if he/she has any financial interest in the referral service.
criteria.guideline: Surveyors must pay particular attention to services that are offered in the same facility by different practitioners for example, where a general practitioner refers a patient to a radiology or laboratory practice in the same facility.

criteria.criterion_text: Referrals are to specific individuals and/or agencies in the patient's home community wherever possible.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and as appropriate their families are given follow-up instructions which are provided in an understandable form and manner.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: A copy of the referral note is available in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The referral is made on appropriate stationery or electronically.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The referral contains as a minimum a) – f) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The practice keeps a record of replies received following the referral of patients.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided of the system that is used in the practice to record and act upon replies received from referral practitioners.

criteria.criterion_text: There are written guidelines for the referral of emergency patients.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Follow-up care based on the findings of investigations/consultations performed outside the practice is noted in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: The practice has an effective clinical handover system that ensures safe and continuing healthcare delivery for patients.
standards.intent:
Clinical handover needs to occur whenever there is an interface of care by different providers.
Examples of clinical handover include:
A medical practitioner covering for a fellow medical practitioner who is on leave or is unexpectedly absent
A medical practitioner covering for a part time colleague
A medical practitioner handing over care to another health professional such as a practice nurse, physiotherapist, podiatrist or psychologist
A medical practitioner referring a patient to a service outside the practice
A shared care arrangement (e.g. team care of a patient with mental health problems)
After hours' care providers are at a disadvantage with regards to specific groups of patients requiring special attention such as chronically ill patients. To ensure optimum care provision and a consistent approach from all clinicians, it is to the advantage of both patients and clinicians if relevant information is made available to the clinicians providing after hours' care.
The sharing of information relating to infection control (e.g. HIV, TB, Hep B, MRSA) will enable those providing care outside the practice (after hours, secondary care, etc.) to offer the patient and persons coming into contact with the affected patient appropriate care and enable them to take precautionary measures.
To ensure the transfer of this information, the patient is provided with a letter to present to clinicians providing care outside the practice.

criteria.criterion_text: There is implementation of a documented policy on clinical handover to ensure that standard processes are followed.
criteria.guideline: The complexity and comprehensiveness of the content of handover information required to achieve continuity of care will differ for different types of handover situations. The details to be included in each situation should be agreed upon by all stakeholders and formalised in a practice to ensure a standardised approach at all times.  The transfer of information must be documented, preferably using standardised forms (electronic or paper-based) for the various handovers. Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients must be involved in decision making regarding the handing over of clinical information.
criteria.guideline: Patients also have a responsibility for their own health and must be involved in the decision of who will continue with their care. Compliance will be verified by documentation that the patient has been informed and shared in the decision of handing over.

criteria.criterion_text: Handover of clinical information is recorded in the patients' health records.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Information relating to those patients requiring special attention, such as the chronically ill or those with infections which require specific infection control measures, is provided in a patient held letter, a copy of which is kept in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: The practice ensures safe and reasonable arrangements for clinical care for patients outside of normal opening or operational hours.
standards.intent:
Medical care outside normal opening or operational hours needs to be provided by recognised service provider.
The method of providing such care can be achieved in one of the following ways:
The practice's service provider provide their own care for patients outside normal opening hours, either individually or through a roster
Formal arrangements for cooperative care outside the normal opening hours exist through a cooperative of one or more local practices
Formal arrangements exist with a medical deputising (locum) service
Formal arrangements exist with a local hospital or after hours' facility
When the practice's service provider cannot safely or reasonably deliver care outside normal opening or operational hours, the practice can clearly document the alternative system of care that is available for their patients.
The practice is responsible for ensuring the alternative care provider offers care to an acceptable standard.

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence of one (or a combination) of arrangements for patients to access care outside of normal opening hours.
criteria.guideline: Regardless of the arrangements used to provide care outside normal opening hours, documented evidence of the system the practice uses to provide such care needs to be available.

criteria.criterion_text: The credentials of alternative care providers are verified.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient health records contain reports or notes of investigations occurring outside normal opening hours by or on behalf of the practice.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will not be applicable to consultations that occur at public health facilities. Evidence must be provided on how and when the practice receives notes of investigations occurring through all other after-hours arrangements. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: The practice provides emergency treatment when necessary.
standards.intent: Emergency treatment of patients may be unpredictable, particularly in practices that do not form part of a 24-hour service. The practice must be prepared to provide primary emergency treatment as required to stabilise a patient while waiting for further assistance from emergency medical services.

criteria.criterion_text: Written guidelines are available and followed relating to the provision of primary emergency services.
criteria.guideline: As a minimum, the Basic Life Support algorithm must be available and followed. Documented evidence of training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Guidelines for emergency triage, assessment and treatment are available and followed for all types of patients seen at the practice (for example adult, paediatric and neonatal).
criteria.guideline: The practice’s policy on triage must make distinction between triage undertaken by members of the clinical team and triage undertaken by personnel with non-clinical roles. Appropriate training must be provided to assist both administrative personnel and members of the clinical team to identify patients in need or urgent care. Documented evidence of training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Information on cases and the outcome of emergency treatment are recorded in a register/logbook.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: Case reviews are undertaken within the practice to assess the quality of treatment and care of patients requiring emergency care.
criteria.guideline: This can take the form of morbidity and mortality meetings, debriefing sessions and peer reviews. Documented evidence must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a protocol that delineates how the practice evaluates, manages, stabilises and transfers patients with emergency conditions.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

standards.standard_title: The practice provides resuscitation in accordance with practice policy.
standards.intent:
Resuscitation policies and equipment should be standardised throughout the practice.
Standardised resuscitation processes require:
The level at which resuscitation is provided and by whom
Coordination among those who provide and maintain the equipment
Availability of required equipment
Availability of required drugs
Initial and ongoing training of personnel in the use of equipment and execution of procedures
Maintenance and monitoring of equipment
Current, evidence-based guidelines for resuscitation
Deficiencies in the system regarding equipment, its use, knowledge and skills required by those who carry out resuscitation should be identified, documented and acted upon. Each practice should identify those members of personnel to be trained in emergency life support and the level of training (basic or advanced) appropriate to their role in the practice.  The person(s) providing the training must be currently registered and/or accredited with a recognised body as a resuscitation trainer. Training in many instances can be outsourced.

criteria.criterion_text: The practice has a policy on resuscitation, which includes the level at which resuscitation is provided, by whom and training and equipment requirements.
criteria.guideline: Documented policies and procedures that include at least a) – g) must be available and implemented. Evidence of implementation of all of the listed policies is required for a compliance rating, otherwise this criterion will be scored PC.

criteria.criterion_text: There are identified personnel trained in basic resuscitation techniques at least every two years, with records of their attendance at such training.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of nationally accepted training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: All medical practitioners are updated in medication protocols for resuscitation and resuscitation techniques at least every two years with records of their attendance at such training.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of nationally accepted training must be provided.

criteria.criterion_text: A designated person documents daily checks to ensure that resuscitation medication and equipment is available, in working order and that medication is not expired and reports any adverse findings for immediate remediation.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence must be provided.

standards.standard_title: Equipment for resuscitation is available in accordance with the policies of the practice.
standards.intent:
Resuscitation equipment and medication is available according to the practice's policy and protocol. There is access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within 3 minutes of any patient collapsing. Resuscitation equipment includes at least:
a bag-mask manual ventilator
a selection of Guedel airways

criteria.criterion_text: There is a designated resuscitation area.
criteria.guideline: This does not have to be an area that is used exclusively for resuscitation, it may be in the normal consulting area. The area must however allow easy access for resuscitation equipment and emergency personnel, have a suitable surface on which to perform resuscitation and ensure the privacy of the patient.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a mechanism for the summoning of medical help in an emergency.
criteria.guideline: This refers to the summoning of medical help both from within the practice itself (to alert personnel that there is an emergency) and from outside the practice. Various methods such as an alarm, intercom, alert button, telephone etc. acceptable. Personnel must be able to demonstrate the procedure to be followed when an emergency situation arises.

criteria.criterion_text: The practice provides resuscitation equipment according to the practices emergency response policy.
criteria.guideline: Resuscitation equipment and documented evidence of the checking thereof according to the practice’s resuscitation policy must be available but must include at least a bag-mask manual ventilator and a selection of Guedel airways. Checking must include the identification of expiry dates on this equipment. Documented evidence of this checking is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Diagnostic and vital sign monitoring equipment is available as per practice policy.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured on physical inspection.

criteria.criterion_text: Equipment for early cardiopulmonary resuscitation is available within one minute in each area of the practice and access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within three minutes of any patient collapsing.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: The practice has access to Ambulance Services (EMS).
criteria.guideline: The practice personnel must be able to describe the process for contacting ambulance services and the contact details must be readily available at all telephones in the practice.

standards.standard_title: A radiation safety programme is in place, followed and documented.
standards.intent:
The practice has an active radiation safety programme appropriate to the risks and hazards encountered. The programme addresses safety practices and prevention measures for radiation personnel, other personnel, patients and members of the public. Where the practice is within the setting of a hospital, the programme is coordinated with the hospital's safety management programme.
The radiation safety programme includes:
Documented policies and procedures which support compliance with applicable standards, laws and regulations
Documented policies and procedures for the handling and disposal of infectious and hazardous materials
The availability of safety protective devices appropriate to the practices and hazards encountered
The orientation of all nuclear medicine personnel to safety procedures and practices
In-service education for new procedures and newly-acquired or recognised hazardous materials

criteria.criterion_text: A radiation safety programme is in place and is appropriate to the risks and hazards encountered.
criteria.guideline: Each practice should have a documented radiation safety programme which is known to and implemented by all personnel, including non-radiation workers. Compliance will be verified by direct observation and documented evidence.

criteria.criterion_text: The programme is coordinated with the practice safety management programme, where applicable.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented records of radioactive stocks, calculation and preparation, administration and disposal details are kept.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

criteria.criterion_text: A register is kept of sealed sources.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence is required.

standards.standard_title: A suitably qualified radiation safety officer is responsible for managing the radiation safety programme.
standards.intent:
The diagnostic imaging service must be under the direction of an individual who is qualified by virtue of documented training, expertise and experience, in accordance with applicable laws and regulations.   This individual must assume professional responsibility for implementing the radiation safety programme
The radiation safety officer's responsibilities should include:
Developing, implementing and maintaining policies and procedures relating to the radiation safety programme
Maintaining any relevant quality control programmes
Monitoring and reviewing all radiation safety incidents
Supporting and advising personnel in the event of radiation incidents.

criteria.criterion_text: A radiation technologist/medical physicist, who is qualified by education, training and experience, CRITICAL manages the radiation safety programme.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific requirements need to be taken into consideration and the required evidence can be obtained from various sources such as post specifications, or job description.  Where applicable, there should be evidence of registration with the relevant professional body/council.

criteria.criterion_text: The responsibilities of this person include developing, implementing and maintaining relevant policies and procedures.
criteria.guideline: These responsibilities should be defined in the job description and will be verified by reviewing personnel records.

criteria.criterion_text: The responsibilities of this person include maintaining quality control programmes.
criteria.guideline: The initial commissioning tests should be done by the supplier.  Thereafter the routine tests should be carried out by the licence holder or persons appointed by the licence holder.  Tests must be carried out in accordance with country-specific legislation, where applicable.  When a new unit is installed, acceptance tests must be performed by the supplier. With regards to regular monitoring, the minimum requirements would be monitoring of the processor, Entrance Skin Exposure (ESE) measurements on diagnostic units, DAP measurements, radiation output repeatability, x-ray tube and generator checks.

criteria.criterion_text: The responsibilities of this person include supporting and advising personnel in the event of radiation incidents.
criteria.guideline: A record of all radiation incidents must be kept and analysed in order to identify any trends in working patterns, poor radiation safety practices and repeated high doses amongst individual employees. Compliance will be verified by direct observation and documented evidence.

criteria.criterion_text: The responsibilities of this person include monitoring and reviewing all radiation safety incidents.
criteria.guideline: Monitoring systems which review the effective implementation of the radiation safety programme should be implemented. These can include regular staff meetings, and should also include the quality control and radiation safety aspects

standards.standard_title: There are documented policies and procedures to guide personnel in all aspects of radiation safety within the practice.
standards.intent: Documented policies and procedures are essential to guide personnel in the diagnostic imaging service in their activities.  The existence of documented procedures does not preclude modifications in the best interests of the patient.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented policies and procedures that address compliance with applicable standards, laws and regulations are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Policies and procedures should include at least the following:  a) The qualifications of staff, b) Radiation safety, including radiation safety inspections, signage in the department, the handling and disposal of hazardous material, over-exposure, the wearing of dosimeters, etc. c) X-rays of pregnant women, Requesting X-rays, Reporting on films including the time frames for reporting, and The availability of emergency drugs and equipment.

criteria.criterion_text: A copy of the local rules relating to current ionising radiation regulations is available.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific arrangements will apply. Where the country regulations are silent on the rules, guidelines should be sought from international radiation protection organisations such as the International Commission for Radiation Protection (www.icrp.org).

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures that relate to limiting the irradiation of patients to levels consistent with medical requirements are implemented. The ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable) principle is applied to the calculation, preparation and administration of radioactive doses.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific requirements need to be taken into consideration. Where the country regulations are silent on the rules, guidelines should be sought from international radiation protection organisations such as the International Commission for Radiation Protection (www.icrp.org). The required evidence can be obtained from documented policies and procedures and the implementation thereof will be verified through observation and review of patient records.

criteria.criterion_text: A strict policy on the conditions under which pregnant women may be subjected to a procedure involving radiation exposure is available and implemented.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific requirements need to be taken into consideration. Where the country regulations are silent on the rules, guidelines should be sought from international radiation protection organisations such as the International Commission for Radiation Protection (www.icrp.org). The required evidence can be obtained from documented policies and procedures and the implementation thereof will be verified through observation and review of patient records.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a procedure to ensure professional handling of a radiation emergency situation.
criteria.guideline: This should be outlined in a documented procedure which is dated, regularly reviewed and signed by all staff who have been orientated to the practice.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures that relate to avoiding radioactive contamination, and controlling spread should it occur, are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific requirements need to be taken into consideration. Where the country regulations are silent on the rules, guidelines should be sought from international radiation protection organisations such as the International Commission for Radiation Protection (www.icrp.org). The required evidence can be obtained from documented policies and procedures and the implementation thereof will be verified through observation and review of patient records.

criteria.criterion_text: A documented procedure is available for personnel to follow in the event of radioactive contamination.
criteria.guideline: This should be outlined in a documented procedure which is dated, regularly reviewed and signed by all staff who have been orientated to the practice.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies relating to monitoring the hands, clothing and body of every member of personnel leaving a controlled area are implemented.
criteria.guideline: This should be outlined in a documented procedure which is dated, regularly reviewed and signed by all staff working in controlled areas. The policies should form part of the practice’s local rules and radiation safety programme to which all staff should be orientated.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures are implemented for clinical trials, where applicable.
criteria.guideline: Where the practice participates in clinical trials, the required documentation should be available. This includes documented standard operating procedures, signed consent forms, approval from the applicable research and ethics committee. Implementation of procedures will be verified by review of patient records.

standards.standard_title: Testing and maintenance are related to the use of the equipment and its documented history of service.
standards.intent: All items of radiation based equipment must be tested regularly and maintained to a high standard to ensure it is safe for use in patients and do not pose any risk to staff and the environment by way of radiation leaks, faulty mechanisms, etc. Regular quality control checks must be conducted at regular intervals as specified by the manufacturer and the results recorded.

criteria.criterion_text: The radiation safety programme includes monitoring and follow-up of radiation based equipment.
criteria.guideline: The initial commissioning tests should be done by the supplier. Thereafter the routine tests should be carried out by the licence holder or persons appointed by the licence holder.  Tests must be carried out in accordance with country-specific legislation, where applicable.  When a new unit is installed, acceptance tests must be performed by the supplier. With regards to regular monitoring, the minimum requirements would be monitoring of the processor, Entrance Skin Exposure (ESE) measurements on diagnostic units, DAP measurements, radiation output repeatability, x-ray tube and generator checks.

criteria.criterion_text: A copy of the most recent radiation safety inspection report is held by the physician responsible for the practice, or the medical physics department or the medical physicist.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  The radiation safety report should be current, reflect the practice name and number and the responsible persons. The report should also list all radiation equipment to which the report refers to which will be checked and verified against existing equipment in operation.

criteria.criterion_text: The construction of the building; in terms of walls, ceilings, floors, doors and windows; which houses radiation based units and equipment has the appropriate structural shielding.
criteria.guideline: The procedure rooms housing radiation based equipment must be adequately shielded. For example, procedure rooms should be lead lined, including the doors, walls adjacent to uncontrolled waiting areas should have the necessary shielding so that doses received by workers and members of the public are kept to the minimum and shall not exceed the respective annual effective doses as prescribed by the relevant Radiation Protection Authority. Compliance will be measured by observation and building regulation certifications.

criteria.criterion_text: Radiation monitors are calibrated regularly.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of calibration records must be available.

criteria.criterion_text: Values are recorded in a log-book.
criteria.guideline: The log-book must be available and up to date.

criteria.criterion_text: There is adequate documentation of all testing, maintenance and calibration of radiation based equipment.
criteria.guideline: Documentation must be available.

criteria.criterion_text: All radiation based equipment is checked on a daily basis.
criteria.guideline: The log-book for each item of radiation based equipment must be available, completed according to departmental policy, and up to date.

criteria.criterion_text: Regular and frequent quality control procedures (e.g. flood uniformities, centre-of-rotation) are attended to or supervised by the medical physicist.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of appropriate action taken in response to abnormal quality control results with confirmation of the effectiveness of the action must also be made available for assessment.

standards.standard_title: The practice ensures the safe, efficient and effective functioning of the service where radiation exposure is concerned.
standards.intent: All practice personnel work with the radiation safety officer and the management team to ensure that facilities provide for safety and that they comply with current radiation safety laws and regulations.

criteria.criterion_text: Radiation warning signs are displayed where radioactive material are handled
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation. This includes the entrances to all imaging rooms, items of radiation equipment, ‘hotlabs’, and toilets designated for patients who have received radioactivity.

criteria.criterion_text: Requirements regarding controlled and supervised areas laid down by the Radiation Protection Inspectorate/Board or other authority are implemented.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a shower available in the event of contamination.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Separate toilets for personnel and patients are available.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: A radioactive spill kit is available within the practice (where applicable) and contains as a minimum: Personal Protective Equipment, absorbent sheets for liquid spills, radioactive waste bags/containers, demarcating warning signs, decontamination materials.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Facilities are available for the quality control of all radiopharmaceutical kits reconstituted on the premises.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: All containers with radioactivity are labelled according to specifications stating that the contents are radioactive and indicating the activity and the date.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: There is documented evidence of radioactive waste disposal which includes the date of disposal, the radioisotope including the half-life, the quantity of radioisotope disposed of and the method of disposal.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation and documented evidence..

criteria.criterion_text: Contamination monitors are provided.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

criteria.criterion_text: Area monitors are available where necessary.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified by direct observation.

standards.standard_title: The practice implements radiation safety practices relevant to occupational and public exposure.
standards.intent:
The ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principle should be applied to diagnostic imaging    examinations in order to obtain optimal images, while keeping the radiation dose as low as possible.    The WHO identifies factors to minimise radiation dose to patients and personnel that are within the    control of the radiographer, which include the following:
Limitation of field size to the area of interest
Use of fast screen-film combinations whenever appropriate
Optimal film processing
Use of automatic exposure times if available
Use of gonad shields
Selection of grid
Compression of obese patients
Highest practicable kV and lowest mAs
Reduction of the number of repeat images by careful patient positioning and use of immobilisation    devices
Performance of basic quality assurance tests
No continuous radiation during fluoroscopy
Field size to be smaller than screen size during fluoroscopy
Only required personnel allowed into the room during radiographic examinations
X-ray units must have adequate shielding
All personnel should stand behind a protective barrier during exposure
Personnel who are required outside the barrier must wear lead-rubber aprons
Personnel should stand outside the path of the primary beam and as far away as possible
Lead-rubber flaps to be used on image intensifiers to reduce scatter to personnel

criteria.criterion_text: The practice ensures that radiation to patient's personnel is kept as low as possible.
criteria.guideline: Dose levels are monitored for all staff members and investigations done when individual levels are suspiciously high or reach the classified worker limit.

criteria.criterion_text: The required radiation safety devices are made available for use by staff and patients and are in good condition.
criteria.guideline: Appropriate radiation safety devices are available which provide the necessary shielding required for the type of radiation utilised in the service. Compliance will be verified by direct observation. This includes, lead aprons, thyroid shields, gonad shields, lead syringe shields, lead dose carriers, etc.

criteria.criterion_text: All personnel are oriented to all radiation safety procedures and practices as part of the radiation safety programme.
criteria.guideline: The practice should provide evidence of training for all radiation safety procedures. This training should form part of the radiation safety programme, to which all new staff should be orientated. Training records should include the details of training, the name of staff and the date of training.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented policy for reporting, investigating, and responding to patient care mismatching events when they occur and implementing changes, where relevant, to reduce the risk of unnecessary radiation exposure to patients.
Personnel receive education with regard to new procedures, equipment and newly-acquired hazardous materials.
criteria.guideline: The practice should provide evidence of training for all new protocols and standard operating procedures, equipment and materials. This training should form part of the radiation safety programme, to which all new staff should be orientated. Training records should include the details of training, the name of staff and the date of training.

criteria.criterion_text: Personal dosimeters worn by personnel comply with the ionising radiation regulations.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Dosimeter badges should be available for all radiation workers. The register for radiation workers needs to be available and up to date. Every registered radiation worker should be supplied with a dosimeter. This dosimeter must be replaced every 32 days, unless a radiation occurrence is suspected or has been established. All full-time theatre personnel must be monitored. In cases where the workload is very low e.g. every three to four months, these staff members may be exempted from being monitored. A file should be available with dosimeter reading reports for all registered persons.

criteria.criterion_text: Signs warning of the dangers of radiation to pregnant and breast-feeding women are prominently displayed.
criteria.guideline: Although every female patient of reproductive age should have the date of her last menstrual period recorded on the request form and also asked of the possibility of pregnancy prior to high-risk procedures in order to avert tragedy, these signs will serve as an additional prompt for patients who may be pregnant to alert personnel in the diagnostic imaging service of this fact.

criteria.criterion_text: Documented systems are in place to ensure that pregnant radiographers/technicians are provided with additional radiation monitoring devices and are restricted to work in low risk areas.
criteria.guideline: Female personnel of reproductive age must be informed of departmental policy in relation to pregnancy as part of their orientation to the department.

criteria.criterion_text: There are appropriate safety signs in place where access must be restricted. These include, where applicable, all radiation preparation, imaging, treatment and storage rooms, Magnetic Resonance Imaging rooms
criteria.guideline: The provision of such safety features will be assessed during the visit to each practice.
